subject: chem_sl
analysis: Full marks: the answer gave both required distinctions — an element contains one type of atom, while a compound contains two or more types chemically combined.

textbook_ref.quote: atoms and elements are represented by the same symbols

subject: chem_sl
analysis: The MS wanted the data-booklet NUMBERS: electronegativity difference ΔΧ = 0.8 AND average Χ = 2.2. Just saying 'there is a difference in electronegativity' scores nothing. When a question says 'Use sections 9 and 17 of the data booklet', it is telling you the mark depends on quoting those values. The average Χ ≈ 2.2 is what places FeS in the polar-covalent region, and ΔΧ = 0.8 shows the bond is polar.

textbook_ref.quote: electronegativity difference ... character covalent bonding

subject: chem_sl
analysis: Anode (left) and cathode (right) were labelled correctly = 1 mark, but the question also asked to 'show the direction of ion movement' and that was left off. In electrolysis of molten FeS the anion S2- migrates to the anode (left) and the cation Fe2+ to the cathode (right). The 4 markable items were: left=anode, right=cathode, S2-→left, Fe2+→right; you showed 2 of 4.

textbook_ref.quote: Electrolytic cells ... an electrochemical cell

subject: chem_sl
analysis: Full marks: both electrode half-equations balance atoms and charge, place electrons on the correct side, and match the molten FeS electrolysis shown.

textbook_ref.quote: half-equations helps to show the transfer of electrons

subject: chem_sl
analysis: 'Iron is solid, iron(II) sulfide is aqueous' is factually wrong — both are solids, and 'state' is not a distinguishing physical property here. You needed a real physical-property contrast of a metal (Fe) vs an ionic solid (FeS): e.g. Fe conducts electricity / is malleable / is magnetic / denser / higher melting point, AND FeS is not. Do NOT use chemical properties or molar mass.

textbook_ref.quote: crystalline and brittle ... characteristic of ionic compounds

subject: chem_sl
analysis: Full marks: Co loses its two 4s electrons before 3d electrons, giving Co2+ = [Ar] 3d7; the full configuration written is equivalent.

textbook_ref.quote: build up the electrons in successive orbitals

subject: chem_sl
analysis: Full marks: the equilibrium expression has CoCl4(2-) in the numerator and [Co2+][Cl-]^4 in the denominator, with the coefficient 4 correctly used as an exponent.

textbook_ref.quote: the value of the equilibrium constant depends on temperature

subject: chem_sl
analysis: Full marks: adding NaCl increases chloride concentration, shifting the equilibrium right, while K remains unchanged because temperature is constant.

textbook_ref.quote: shift to counteract the change and return the system to equilibrium

subject: chem_sl
analysis: Full marks: heating shifts the equilibrium toward the blue CoCl4(2-) side, so heat acts as a reactant and the forward reaction is endothermic.

textbook_ref.quote: Temperature is the only factor that affects both the position of equilibrium

subject: chem_sl
analysis: Confirmed: products drawn above reactants (endothermic), so Ea and ΔH both scored (M2, M3). The lost mark (M1) is because reactants and products levels were NOT labelled — you labelled only Ea and ΔH. The question named FOUR things to label; label all of them next time. This was a completeness slip, not a chemistry error.

textbook_ref.quote: the activation energy (Ea), and the enthalpy change for the reaction

subject: chem_sl
analysis: Balancing was correct (CaCO3 + 2HCl -> CaCl2 + CO2 + H2O) = 1 mark. But the question said 'including state symbols' and the MS bundled state symbols into the first mark: CaCO3(s) + 2HCl(aq) -> CaCl2(aq) + CO2(g) + H2O(l). No state symbols = M1 lost. Normally IB ignores state symbols, but when the question explicitly asks for them they are worth a mark — always add (s)/(l)/(g)/(aq) when told to.

textbook_ref.quote: State symbols commonly used ... solid s liquid l gas g aqueous aq

subject: chem_sl
analysis: Root cause confirmed as an arithmetic slip: you got n(CaCO3) = 0.0046 mol, but 3.162 / 100.09 = 0.0316 mol. The MS awards its first mark only when BOTH n(CaCO3)=0.0316 AND n(HCl)=0.0800 are correct, so the wrong value cost that mark; the second mark ('CaCO3 is limiting') was also missed. Two habits to fix: (1) sanity-check a division — 3.16/100 is roughly 0.03, so 0.0046 should have looked wrong; (2) to compare, divide each amount by its coefficient (HCl/2 = 0.0400) and the SMALLER is limiting -> 0.0316 < 0.0400, so CaCO3 is limiting. n(HCl)=0.0800 was correct.

textbook_ref.quote: the limiting reactant allows us to determine the extent of reaction

subject: chem_sl
analysis: Awarded by ECF from 3b — method V = n x 22.7 dm3/mol is correct. Note the 'true' answer (with CaCO3 limiting) is 0.0316 x 22.7 = 0.717 dm3.

textbook_ref.quote: at STP, the molar volume of an ideal gas

subject: chem_sl
analysis: Full marks: the higher-temperature curve starts at the origin, has a greater initial gradient, and reaches the same final gas volume because the reactant amount is unchanged.

textbook_ref.quote: volume of oxygen gas released ... as a function of time

subject: chem_sl
analysis: Full marks: both marking points were stated — the catalyst provides an alternative pathway and that pathway has lower activation energy.

textbook_ref.quote: a catalyst ... increases the rate ... but is not consumed

subject: chem_sl
analysis: Full marks: the oxidation half-equation balances S, O, H and charge, and combining it with four Fe3+ reduction half-equations gives the correct overall redox equation.

textbook_ref.quote: half-equations helps to show the transfer of electrons

subject: chem_sl
analysis: Left blank. Both definitions hinge on the phrase 'electrostatic attraction'. Ionic bonding = electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged ions (cations AND anions). Covalent bonding = electrostatic attraction between a shared pair of electrons and the (two) positively charged nuclei. Do NOT describe ionic bonding as 'transfer of electrons' — that loses the mark. This is pure recall; worth memorising the two one-liners.

textbook_ref.quote: electrostatic attractions between positively charged species and negatively charged species

subject: chem_sl
analysis: Lewis structure of NO3- correct (one N=O, two N-O, charge shown). Minor: your valence-electron tally was written 3+1+18 (N contributes 5, not 3) though you reached 24 correctly — tidy that up so the count is defensible.

textbook_ref.quote: Covalent bonds are formed when atoms share pairs of valence electrons

subject: chem_sl
analysis: You wrote 'trigonal pyramidal'; the answer is trigonal planar. NO3- has 3 bonding domains on N and NO lone pair on N, so VSEPR gives trigonal planar (120 degrees). Trigonal pyramidal (like NH3) needs 3 bonds PLUS a lone pair on the central atom. Count domains on the central atom first: your own correct Lewis structure in 4b(ii) had no lone pair on N, so it must be planar.

textbook_ref.quote: 3 trigonal planar 0 trigonal planar ... 1 bent

subject: chem_sl
analysis: Full marks: the carbon and hydrogen amounts, oxygen by mass difference, and final simplest whole-number ratio all match the markscheme, giving C3H8O.

textbook_ref.quote: empirical formula shows the simplest ratio of atoms

subject: chem_sl
analysis: Full marks: temperature and volume were converted to SI units before applying pV = nRT, producing 0.00180 mol.

textbook_ref.quote: the ideal gas equation ... pV=nRT

subject: chem_sl
analysis: Full marks: dividing 0.108 g by the amount from the previous part gives 60.0 g mol-1, matching the accepted markscheme range.

textbook_ref.quote: Molar mass ... is the mass of 1 mol
